enrichment.short_description: FlatPress is a free, open-source flat-file blogging engine written in PHP that stores every post, comment, and setting as plain files instead of in a database, making it a lightweight option for self-hosted blogs.
enrichment.seo_title: FlatPress Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FlatPress review: a free, database-free PHP blogging engine. See how its flat-file architecture, plugins, pricing and alternatives compare in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
FlatPress is a lightweight, open-source blogging engine written entirely in PHP that does away with the database layer most content management systems depend on. Instead of storing posts, comments, categories, and configuration in a relational database like MySQL, FlatPress writes everything to plain files on disk, organized into a predictable folder structure. This 'flat-file' approach is the defining characteristic of the project and the source of its name: content lives as files, not as rows in database tables. The result is a CMS that can run on almost any shared hosting environment, including low-cost plans that do not provide database access, and that can be backed up or migrated simply by copying its directory to another location.

The project traces its roots back to 2006. At the time, PHP hosting with reliable, always-available database access was far less universal than it is today, and a number of early bloggers and developers were experimenting with alternatives to database-backed blogging tools. The team behind FlatPress set out to build a blogging engine from scratch that would store content purely in the filesystem, while borrowing conceptual ideas from WordPress, most notably its plugin and hook system, to keep the software extensible. The name FlatPress reflects that combination directly: 'flat' for flat-file storage and 'Press' as a nod to the broader blogging-engine lineage it grew out of. The project has been maintained continuously since then by a community of volunteer contributors, with its source now hosted publicly on GitHub under the flatpressblog organization. It is released under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2), which guarantees that anyone can use, study, modify, and redistribute the software freely, including for commercial purposes, as long as derivative works remain under a compatible license.

Technically, FlatPress runs on any standard web server capable of executing PHP, including Apache, Nginx, and IIS, and the current stable line supports PHP versions from 7.2 through 8.5. Content rendering is handled through the Smarty templating engine, which separates presentation logic from the PHP application code and allows theme authors to build and swap visual designs without touching the core engine. On top of the templating layer, FlatPress exposes a plugin and widget architecture that lets developers hook into posting, commenting, and page-rendering events to add new functionality, similar in spirit to how WordPress plugins attach to actions and filters, though implemented independently and scoped to a much smaller, single-user use case. Administration happens through a web-based admin panel where an author writes and edits entries, manages pages, moderates comments, and configures plugins and themes, all without ever needing to touch a database console or write SQL. Comments come with built-in spam-mitigation tooling, and the software ships with support for more than twenty interface languages, reflecting a long history of community-contributed translations.

Because it is fully open source and community-maintained rather than commercially operated, FlatPress has no pricing tiers, subscription plans, or paid upgrade path. Downloading, installing, and running FlatPress costs nothing beyond whatever web hosting the operator chooses to pay for. There is no official managed or hosted version sold by a company behind the project; every deployment is self-hosted by the person or organization running it, and support comes from the project's documentation wiki, its GitHub issue tracker, and community forums rather than from a paid vendor support desk.

The software is aimed squarely at a specific kind of user: individual bloggers, developers, and small site owners who want a simple, dependency-light publishing tool and who value owning their content outright, in a format they can read, edit, or move without any database export step. It is a natural fit for constrained hosting environments, air-gapped or minimal server setups, and for anyone who wants to keep a long-running personal blog with minimal maintenance overhead. It is explicitly a single-author-oriented tool rather than a multi-editor newsroom platform, and it is not designed to compete with full-featured, plugin-marketplace-driven systems on raw feature count.

Within the broader flat-file CMS category, FlatPress sits alongside other database-free publishing tools such as Bludit, Grav, Pico CMS, Kirby, and HTMLy, all of which share the same core pitch of avoiding a database dependency. Compared to those newer entrants, FlatPress differentiates itself primarily through longevity and simplicity: it has been in continuous development since 2006, making it one of the older surviving projects in the flat-file CMS space, and it deliberately keeps its feature surface narrow and its codebase small rather than expanding into the page-builder or headless-CMS territory that some competitors have moved toward. Against WordPress, by far the dominant blogging and CMS platform overall, FlatPress trades WordPress's enormous plugin and theme marketplace, multi-author workflows, and e-commerce integrations for a much smaller footprint, no database administration burden, and a codebase simple enough for a single developer to fully understand.

On GitHub, the flatpressblog/flatpress repository has accumulated a modest but active community footprint, with over two hundred stars, dozens of forks, and ongoing releases; the most recent stable release at the time of writing is version 1.5.1. The project maintains automated code-quality tooling, including static analysis via PHPStan and security scanning via CodeQL, indicating an ongoing, if unhurried, maintenance cadence rather than an abandoned codebase. Its own project blog, used partly as a live demonstration of the software, has hosted more than 220 entries without any reported performance degradation, which the maintainers point to as evidence that the flat-file model holds up reasonably well for a typical personal-blog volume of content, even though very large, high-traffic, thousands-of-posts sites would generally be better served by a database-backed system with indexed queries.

Architecturally, a FlatPress installation consists of the PHP application code, a set of theme files rendered through Smarty, an optional set of plugins, and a content directory where posts and their metadata are stored as files rather than database rows. There is no separate database server process to install, configure, patch, or back up separately from the application; the entire state of a blog, content, comments, and settings included, lives inside the same directory tree as the application itself. That property is also what makes migrating a FlatPress site between hosts unusually simple: copying the folder is functionally equivalent to a full backup and restore.

In terms of market position, FlatPress occupies a small but durable niche rather than competing for mainstream CMS market share. It is not venture-backed, has no enterprise sales motion, and is not positioned to challenge WordPress, Ghost, or hosted blogging platforms like Substack for general-purpose blogging market share. Instead, its continued relevance comes from a steady, if niche, audience of developers and hobbyists who specifically want a no-database, self-contained, GPL-licensed blogging tool they can run on minimal infrastructure and fully control. Over roughly two decades, the project's evolution has been incremental rather than transformative: successive releases have focused on keeping pace with modern PHP versions, refining the plugin and theming system, hardening security, and maintaining translations, rather than pivoting the product toward new categories such as headless content delivery or e-commerce. That steady, conservative development pace is itself part of the project's identity and part of why it continues to attract users who prioritize stability and simplicity over feature breadth.

Security and reliability practices around FlatPress reflect the project's small-team, community-driven nature rather than a formal enterprise security program. Because the codebase is public on GitHub, vulnerabilities and bugs are reported and tracked through standard GitHub issues, and the automated CodeQL scanning integrated into the repository's continuous-integration pipeline provides an ongoing layer of static analysis aimed at catching common PHP security issues, such as improper input handling, before they reach a tagged release. This transparency is itself a form of assurance for administrators who want to audit exactly what code is running their blog, something not possible with closed-source alternatives. Operationally, running FlatPress safely still requires the same baseline precautions as any self-hosted PHP application: keeping the PHP runtime patched, restricting file permissions on the content directory, and applying FlatPress's own point releases promptly, since, unlike a managed SaaS blogging platform, there is no vendor automatically patching a hosted instance on the operator's behalf.

Documentation for FlatPress is maintained primarily as a community wiki alongside inline help in the admin panel, covering installation, theme development, plugin development, and upgrade paths between major versions. Because the project has no dedicated technical writing staff, documentation quality and completeness can vary by section and by translation, which is a common trade-off for volunteer-maintained open-source software relative to funded commercial products with dedicated documentation teams. New users are generally expected to have basic familiarity with uploading files to a web server and editing configuration files, since installation is a manual, unzip-and-configure process rather than a one-click hosted signup flow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FlatPress is a free, open-source blogging engine built in PHP that stores all content in flat files instead of a database. First released in 2006, it was designed for hosting environments without reliable database access and has been maintained by a volunteer community ever since, released under the GNU GPLv2 license.

The project borrows conceptually from WordPress in its plugin and hook system, while keeping its own footprint intentionally small: no database server to install or maintain, and a backup process that amounts to copying a single directory.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include flat-file content storage with no database dependency, Smarty-based theming for separating design from application logic, a plugin and widget system for extending functionality, and a web-based admin panel for writing posts and managing comments and pages.
FlatPress supports PHP 7.2 through 8.5, ships with translations in more than twenty languages, and includes built-in comment spam mitigation, making it usable on minimal shared-hosting plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: FlatPress is entirely free and open source under GPLv2, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or official managed hosting offering. The only ongoing cost is whatever web hosting the operator chooses to run it on.
Support comes from the project's documentation wiki, GitHub issue tracker, and community forums rather than a commercial vendor support desk.

faqs.question: Is FlatPress free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. FlatPress is fully open source under the GNU GPLv2 license, with no paid tiers or subscription fees.

faqs.question: Does FlatPress require a database?
faqs.answer: No. FlatPress stores all posts, comments, and settings as flat files, so no MySQL or other database server is needed.

faqs.question: What server requirements does FlatPress have?
faqs.answer: FlatPress runs on Apache, Nginx, or IIS with PHP 7.2 through 8.5, and needs no separate database service.

faqs.question: Is FlatPress still actively maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes. The project continues to release updates on GitHub, with version 1.5.1 as its most recent stable release.

faqs.question: How do I back up a FlatPress site?
faqs.answer: Because all content lives in files rather than a database, a full backup is as simple as copying the site's directory.

faqs.question: Is FlatPress a good choice for large, high-traffic websites?
faqs.answer: FlatPress works best for personal or small blogs; the flat-file model is well tested at moderate content volumes but is not optimized for very large, high-traffic publications the way a database-backed CMS is.

enrichment.short_description: Fleet is an open-source device management platform built on osquery that gives IT and security teams cross-platform MDM, endpoint visibility, and vulnerability management for macOS, Windows, Linux, iOS, iPadOS, Android, and ChromeOS.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (Open Source core plus paid tiers)
enrichment.seo_title: Fleet (FleetDM) Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fleet (FleetDM) review: open-source, osquery-based device management with cross-platform MDM. Compare features, 2026 pricing tiers, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fleet, commercially branded FleetDM, is an open-source device management platform that gives IT and security teams a single console to inventory, monitor, configure, and secure endpoints across nearly every major operating system, including macOS, Windows, Linux, iOS, iPadOS, Android, and ChromeOS. At its technical core, Fleet is built on osquery, an open-source agent that exposes the state of an operating system, its running processes, installed software, network connections, configuration settings, and more, as queryable SQL tables. Fleet acts as the control plane for fleets of osquery agents, letting administrators run live SQL queries across thousands of devices simultaneously, schedule recurring queries for continuous monitoring, and turn the results into policies, alerts, and compliance checks. On top of that osquery-based visibility layer, Fleet has grown into a full mobile device management (MDM) platform, handling device enrollment, configuration profiles, remote lock and wipe, software deployment, and OS update enforcement through native Apple MDM and Windows MDM protocols.

The company's lineage traces directly back to osquery itself. Osquery was created in 2014 at Facebook by Zach Wasserman, Mike Arpaia, and their team, and was later open-sourced, becoming a widely adopted standard for endpoint instrumentation across the security industry. Wasserman, Arpaia, and Jason Meller subsequently founded Kolide and built an open-source control server for osquery deployments known as Kolide Fleet, which made it substantially easier to operate osquery at scale inside an organization. In November 2020, Zach Wasserman partnered with Mike McNeil to spin that project out into a new, independent company, Fleet Device Management Inc., headquartered in San Francisco, California, with McNeil serving as CEO and Wasserman as CTO. The open-source Fleet project and the company built around it have continued to evolve together since that spinout, expanding from an osquery fleet manager into a broader cross-platform MDM and endpoint security product, including a notable 2023 launch positioning Fleet as an industry-first open-source, cross-platform MDM platform spanning both desktop and mobile operating systems.

Technically, a Fleet deployment consists of a central Fleet server, which exposes a web dashboard, a REST API, and a command-line interface, and a lightweight agent called fleetd that runs on each managed device, bundling osquery along with Fleet's own enrollment and MDM logic. Administrators can manage the entire fleet through the UI, through direct API calls, or through Fleet's GitOps workflow, which lets teams define device policies, queries, and configuration as YAML files stored in a Git repository and applied through continuous-integration pipelines, an approach that appeals strongly to engineering-led IT and security teams already comfortable managing infrastructure as code. Fleet supports single sign-on for administrator access, webhooks for integrating alerts into external systems such as Slack or a SIEM, and continuous vulnerability detection that cross-references installed software against known CVEs.

Fleet's pricing follows a freemium, open-core model. The free tier costs nothing per host, is licensed under the MIT license for its core components, and is fully self-hostable, including in air-gapped environments; it includes device inventory, labels, policies, the REST API, webhooks, CLI tooling, GitOps support, SSO, and community support. Fleet Premium, the paid tier, is priced starting at $7 per host per month and unlocks MDM migration tooling, zero-touch enrollment, BYOD (bring-your-own-device) enrollment, conditional access, application deployment, remote script execution, remote lock and wipe, continuous vulnerability scanning, and phone and email support with unlimited coverage. Above Premium, Fleet offers a Custom tier reached by contacting sales, aimed at organizations with large-scale deployments that need tailored terms and dedicated support. Self-hosting is available on every tier, while Fleet's own managed cloud hosting option is currently limited to customers with 700 or more managed hosts, with broader availability described by the company as forthcoming.

Fleet's target audience skews toward engineering-led organizations rather than traditional, non-technical IT departments. Its API-first design, native GitOps support, and SQL-based query interface make it especially well suited to security and DevOps teams that want to treat device management the same way they treat other infrastructure, as version-controlled, auditable code, rather than as a purely point-and-click console experience. Typical users include security operations teams that need deep, real-time endpoint visibility for threat hunting and incident response, IT teams at technology companies managing a heterogeneous fleet of Apple, Windows, and Linux machines from one platform, and compliance-focused organizations that need continuous vulnerability and configuration monitoring across their device estate.

In the broader device management market, Fleet competes against long-established commercial MDM vendors as well as tools descended from its own lineage. Jamf Pro remains the dominant, Apple-focused MDM platform, especially strong in education and enterprise Apple deployments. Microsoft Intune offers cloud-based endpoint management bundled into Microsoft's licensing tiers and is deeply embedded in organizations standardized on Microsoft 365. Kandji, rebranded as Iru in October 2025, targets Apple-centric organizations with a polished, unified management platform. Kolide, the company Fleet's founding team originally came from, continues to operate in an adjacent space focused on device trust and end-user-driven security rather than full MDM. Against all of these, Fleet differentiates itself primarily on being open source with a genuinely usable free tier, on its osquery-powered SQL query model that gives far more granular visibility than typical MDM dashboards, and on GitOps-native configuration that lets device policy live alongside application code in the same version-control workflows engineering teams already use.

Fleet operates as a fully remote, distributed company, with its roughly 85 to 90 employees spread across multiple continents rather than concentrated in a single office, a structure the company documents openly in its public handbook. The business has grown from a niche osquery tooling company into a recognized entrant in the broader unified endpoint management category, reflected in its expansion from Apple-only MDM support to a single console covering Windows, Linux, ChromeOS, iOS, iPadOS, and Android alongside macOS. That expansion mirrors a broader industry trend toward consolidating device management, vulnerability management, and patching into one platform rather than requiring separate point tools for each function.

Architecturally and philosophically, Fleet's core bet is that transparency and extensibility, an auditable, open-source codebase, a documented API, and infrastructure-as-code workflows, are increasingly what security-conscious, engineering-heavy organizations want from their device management vendor, in contrast to the closed, UI-only model of legacy MDM tools. Its open-source repository, hosted publicly on GitHub under the fleetdm organization, remains under active development, with the company continuing to ship features such as expanded Android support, deeper vulnerability management, and broader Windows and Linux MDM capability as it works to close feature gaps with entrenched, Apple-centric or Microsoft-centric incumbents. The product's evolution from a focused osquery fleet manager into a full cross-platform MDM and vulnerability management suite illustrates a deliberate strategy of building outward from a defensible technical core, deep, queryable endpoint telemetry, toward the broader device lifecycle management functions that IT and security buyers expect from a single platform today.

Fleet's public documentation and open handbook, both published on the fleetdm.com website and mirrored in the GitHub repository, are notable for how much of the company's internal operating process is shared openly, including engineering workflows, release procedures, and even elements of company strategy, a transparency practice inherited from the broader open-source and remote-work culture the founding team brought over from their prior roles. This openness extends to Fleet's product roadmap, which is tracked in public GitHub issues and milestones rather than a private backlog, letting customers and community contributors see and influence upcoming features such as expanded Linux MDM support or new vulnerability data sources before they ship. Community contribution is also formally encouraged: external developers can submit pull requests against the open-source core, and Fleet maintains contribution guidelines and a code of conduct consistent with standard open-source project governance.

On the deployment side, Fleet supports installation through Docker Compose for local evaluation, Helm charts for Kubernetes-based production deployments, and Terraform modules for infrastructure-as-code provisioning on AWS, reflecting the cloud-native, DevOps-first orientation of its target customers. The backend is built on Go with a MySQL-compatible database for storage and Redis for caching and live query coordination, a stack chosen for horizontal scalability so that a single Fleet deployment can support very large device fleets, a requirement given that some of its enterprise customers manage tens of thousands of endpoints from a single instance. This technical foundation, combined with the open licensing model, is central to why organizations with strict data residency or air-gapped requirements, such as government contractors or highly regulated financial firms, have adopted Fleet where a purely cloud-only competitor would be disqualified outright.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Fleet (FleetDM) is an open-source device management platform built on osquery that gives IT and security teams SQL-queryable visibility into endpoints alongside full cross-platform MDM for macOS, Windows, Linux, iOS, iPadOS, Android, and ChromeOS.

Founded in November 2020 by CEO Mike McNeil and CTO Zach Wasserman, and headquartered in San Francisco, Fleet grew out of the osquery ecosystem that Wasserman helped create at Facebook in 2014, later building on the Kolide Fleet osquery control server before spinning out as its own company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Fleet combines osquery-based live and scheduled SQL querying of device state with native MDM functionality: enrollment, configuration profiles, remote lock and wipe, software deployment, and OS update enforcement.

The platform is API-first and GitOps-native, letting teams manage device policy as version-controlled YAML, and it supports SSO, webhooks, and self-hosted or air-gapped deployment on every pricing tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Fleet's core is free and MIT-licensed, self-hostable at no cost with device inventory, policies, the REST API, GitOps, SSO, and community support. Fleet Premium starts at $7 per host per month and adds MDM migration, zero-touch and BYOD enrollment, conditional access, application deployment, script execution, remote lock and wipe, and continuous vulnerability scanning with phone and email support.
A Custom tier is available by contacting sales for large-scale deployments, and Fleet's own managed cloud hosting is currently limited to organizations with 700 or more hosts.

enrichment.starting_price: Free (AGPLv3 open source; commercial license available)
enrichment.seo_title: Flexisip Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flexisip is Belledonne Communications' open source SIP server suite for VoIP proxy routing, presence, push notifications and video conferencing infrastructure.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Flexisip is an open source SIP (Session Initiation Protocol) server suite developed by Belledonne Communications, a French telecommunications software company based in Grenoble. Written primarily in C++ and compliant with RFC 3261, Flexisip functions as a central routing hub for SIP-based communications, handling call setup, user registration, presence, instant messaging, push notifications and multi-party conferencing for VoIP and unified-communications deployments. Unlike a consumer application, Flexisip is infrastructure software: it is installed on Linux servers (or embedded devices) by telecom operators, managed service providers and enterprises to run their own private or public telephony networks, in the same category as Kamailio, OpenSIPS, Asterisk and FreeSWITCH.

Belledonne Communications was founded in 2010 in Grenoble, France, by Simon Morlat and Jehan Monnier, both former Hewlett-Packard engineers. Morlat had already created Linphone, the open source softphone project, back in 2001, making Belledonne Communications effectively the commercial and engineering continuation of that earlier open source effort. The company's flagship SIP proxy service began powering the free linphone.org calling service in 2011, giving Flexisip a real-world production testbed from its earliest days. Flexisip itself was formally released as a standalone, commercially supported SIP proxy server product in 2015, and the company has continued to iterate on it since: video calling was integrated into mobile Linphone clients in 2012, ZRTP call encryption was added in 2014, the LIME end-to-end encrypted messaging library shipped in 2018, post-quantum encryption and expanded videoconferencing capability arrived in 2022, and a dedicated Flexisip Account Manager module for streamlined user provisioning launched in 2023, the same year Belledonne Communications reported reaching roughly two million euros in annual revenue. Today the company employs more than 30 people, with roughly 20 to 22 dedicated software engineers, and continues to publish Flexisip's complete source code on GitHub under the BelledonneCommunications organization.

Technically, Flexisip is built around a modular architecture so that operators can deploy only the pieces they need. The core Proxy Server handles SIP message routing and registrar duties for connecting user agents. A separate Push Notification module implements RFC 8599 to wake mobile SIP clients via Apple's APNs and Google's Firebase Cloud Messaging when the app is backgrounded, which is essential for reliable call and message delivery on iOS and Android. A Presence Server tracks and publishes real-time user availability using SIP SIMPLE and XCAP standards. A Back-to-Back User Agent (B2BUA) component sits between call legs to handle identity translation, media transcoding and encryption bridging between endpoints that use different codecs or security profiles. A Conference Server module supports multi-party audio and video conferencing plus group instant messaging, and a RegEvent Server notifies other domains about registration state changes across federated deployments. Because the codebase has minimal external dependencies and is written in efficient, low-level C++ (targeting C++20 with GCC 13 or later, or Clang 19 or later, and built via CMake), Flexisip can run not only on large clustered server farms but also on small embedded and IoT devices, which is one of the project's notable differentiators versus heavier alternatives.

Pricing for Flexisip follows a dual-license open source model rather than a typical SaaS subscription. The full source code is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3), meaning any organization can download, self-host, modify and run Flexisip at no cost, provided that any modified version offered as a network service is also released under the AGPLv3's copyleft terms. Organizations that want to embed Flexisip into a closed-source commercial product, or that need to avoid the AGPLv3's source-disclosure obligations, can instead purchase a proprietary commercial license directly from Belledonne Communications; pricing for that license is negotiated individually and is not publicly listed, consistent with typical enterprise telecom licensing practices. There is no separate 'freemium' consumer tier because Flexisip is not sold as an end-user product; the free and paid paths both deliver the identical codebase, differing only in licensing terms and the level of vendor support attached.

The target audience for Flexisip is squarely technical and B2B: internet telephony service providers (ITSPs) building carrier-grade calling platforms, managed service providers offering hosted PBX and unified communications to their clients, enterprises that want to run a private, self-hosted calling and messaging network instead of relying on a third-party cloud provider, and hardware or IoT manufacturers who embed SIP calling directly into intercoms, industrial control panels or other connected devices. It pairs naturally with Belledonne Communications' own Linphone softphone and Liblinphone SDK, but because it is a standards-compliant SIP proxy, it also interoperates with any RFC 3261-compliant SIP client or hardware phone, not just Linphone-branded software.

Within the open source telephony space, Flexisip's closest competitors are Kamailio and OpenSIPS, both mature open source SIP proxy/registrar projects with large communities, as well as Asterisk and FreeSWITCH, which are broader PBX and media-server platforms rather than pure SIP proxies. On the commercial cloud side, hosted communications platforms like Twilio's Programmable Voice offer an alternative to self-hosting any SIP infrastructure at all, trading operational control for managed convenience. Flexisip differentiates itself primarily through its tight, first-party integration with the Linphone ecosystem (softphone apps, SDK, and encryption libraries all built by the same company), its emphasis on mobile push notification reliability under RFC 8599, and its lightweight footprint that makes it viable on embedded hardware, an area where Kamailio and OpenSIPS are less commonly deployed.

A few notable facts underscore Flexisip's track record: it has powered the free linphone.org SIP service continuously since 2011, giving it over a decade of production hardening at meaningful scale. Its GitHub mirror carries roughly 185 stars as of mid-2026, a modest but active following typical of specialized telecom infrastructure projects that serve a technical niche rather than a mass developer audience. The project requires relatively modern build tooling (C++20, GCC 13+/Clang 19+, CMake, Python 3, Doxygen) reflecting active, ongoing maintenance rather than legacy stagnation, and it supports both Docker-based and Nix-based reproducible builds for operators who want deterministic deployment pipelines.

Architecturally, Flexisip's design philosophy of separable modules mirrors the broader shift in telecom infrastructure away from monolithic softswitches toward composable microservice-style components, letting operators mix and match a proxy, a presence server, a B2BUA and a conference bridge as independent, independently scalable processes rather than one large binary. This is particularly relevant for cloud-native VoIP deployments where individual components need to scale differently under load, such as a spike in push notification volume that does not require scaling the conferencing subsystem.

In terms of market position, Flexisip occupies a specific niche: it is not attempting to compete with fully managed communications-platform-as-a-service (CPaaS) offerings for developers who want an API and nothing to operate, nor is it a general-purpose PBX like FreePBX built on Asterisk. Instead it serves organizations that specifically want SIP-level control, standards compliance and self-hosting, typically because of data sovereignty requirements, cost control at scale, or the need for deep customization that a closed CPaaS cannot offer. Its evolution from a 2011 in-house proxy powering a single free calling service into a modular, commercially licensable, security-hardened server suite by the mid-2020s reflects Belledonne Communications' broader strategy of monetizing enterprise and OEM licensing on top of a healthy, actively maintained open source core, a model shared by many infrastructure-software companies in the VoIP and real-time communications space.

Beyond the software itself, Belledonne Communications has built a broader ecosystem around Flexisip that reinforces its market position. The company also publishes Mediastreamer2, the media streaming engine used for audio and video processing across its products, and bcg729, an implementation of the G.729 audio codec commonly required for narrowband telephony interoperability. These surrounding libraries mean that an operator adopting Flexisip is not adopting an isolated proxy but a full open source telephony stack, from client SDK to media engine to signaling server, all maintained by a single vendor with over two decades of continuous VoIP engineering experience dating back to the original Linphone project in 2001. For organizations evaluating build-versus-buy decisions around private communications infrastructure, this vertical integration, combined with the option to fall back on a paid commercial license and direct vendor support, is often the deciding factor over adopting a looser combination of separately maintained open source components such as Kamailio plus a third-party media server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Flexisip is an open source SIP proxy and application server suite developed by Belledonne Communications, the French company behind the Linphone softphone. Written in C++ and compliant with RFC 3261, it routes SIP signaling for VoIP calls, instant messaging, presence and conferencing, and can be deployed by telecom operators, enterprises or hardware manufacturers who want to run their own private or public SIP-based communications network.

The project has powered the free linphone.org calling service since 2011 and was released as a standalone commercial product in 2015. It is dual licensed: the full source code is free under AGPLv3, while a proprietary commercial license is available for organizations that need to embed Flexisip into closed-source products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Flexisip's modular architecture separates its capabilities into independent components: a Proxy Server for SIP message routing and registration, a Push Notification module compliant with RFC 8599 for waking mobile clients via APNs and Firebase, a Presence Server for real-time availability, a Back-to-Back User Agent for identity translation and transcoding, and a Conference Server for multi-party audio, video and group messaging.

Because the codebase has minimal dependencies and runs efficiently in C++, Flexisip can be deployed on both large clustered server farms and small embedded or IoT devices, a flexibility that distinguishes it from heavier open source telephony platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Flexisip does not follow a typical SaaS subscription model. Anyone can download, self-host and modify the source code for free under the AGPLv3 license, as long as any modified network-facing service is also released under AGPLv3's copyleft terms.

Organizations that need to distribute Flexisip inside a closed-source product, or that want direct vendor support and access to enterprise modules like the Flexisip Account Manager, can purchase a proprietary commercial license from Belledonne Communications; pricing is negotiated individually and not publicly listed.

faqs.answer: Yes. Flexisip's full source code is released under the AGPLv3 license and can be downloaded, self-hosted and modified at no cost. A separate proprietary commercial license is available for closed-source integration.

faqs.answer: Flexisip is used as SIP proxy and application server infrastructure for VoIP calling, instant messaging, presence, push notifications and conferencing, typically by telecom operators, enterprises and hardware makers.

faqs.answer: Flexisip is developed by Belledonne Communications, a French telecommunications software company founded in 2010 and based in Grenoble, also known for the Linphone softphone.

faqs.question: Does Flexisip support mobile push notifications?
faqs.answer: Yes. Flexisip implements RFC 8599 to deliver push notifications through Apple's APNs and Google's Firebase Cloud Messaging, ensuring reliable call and message delivery to backgrounded mobile apps.

faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Kamailio, OpenSIPS, Asterisk and FreeSWITCH for self-hosted SIP infrastructure, or managed cloud communications platforms such as Twilio for teams that prefer not to self-host.

enrichment.seo_title: Fliki Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fliki is an AI text-to-video and text-to-speech platform that turns scripts and blog posts into narrated videos with AI voices in under two minutes flat.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fliki is an AI-powered text-to-video and text-to-speech content creation platform that lets users generate narrated videos directly from a written script, a blog post URL, a rough idea, or an existing slide deck, without filming, a studio, or manual video-editing skills. The platform matches AI-selected stock footage, images and AI-generated visuals to the input text, pairs it with a synthetic voiceover drawn from a library of AI voices, and assembles a finished, exportable video, typically producing a first draft in under two minutes. It targets solo content creators, marketing teams, educators and businesses that need to produce a high volume of short- and medium-form video content, such as social clips, explainer videos, product walkthroughs, course lessons and localized marketing videos, without the time and cost of traditional video production.

Fliki was founded in 2021 by Sabir Ahmed and Atul Yadav; company records show it is incorporated in Middletown, Delaware, in the United States, while reporting on the founding team indicates a distributed structure with engineering talent based in India, including Bangalore. Public estimates of team size vary across data providers, from roughly three to fifteen people depending on the source and the year measured, but multiple independent write-ups from 2025 describe a lean team of around a dozen to fifteen employees generating meaningful annual revenue, reportedly over a million dollars, which is notable for a company of that size in the crowded AI video-generation category. The company states that Fliki serves more than one million content creators, positioning it as one of the more widely adopted tools in the AI voice-and-video generation space that emerged following the broader wave of generative AI products launched from 2021 onward.

Technically, Fliki's core workflow begins with one of several input modes: idea-to-video, where a user types a topic and the AI drafts a script and matching visuals; script-to-video, where a user pastes their own script; blog-to-video, where a URL is ingested and repurposed into video form; and PPT-to-video, which converts an existing slide deck into a narrated video. Once text is in the system, Fliki's text-to-speech engine renders it using one of over a thousand AI voices spanning more than 80 languages, including a subset of 'ultra-realistic' voices built on more advanced neural text-to-speech models, and the platform automatically synchronizes matching stock media, AI-generated images, or, on higher tiers, AI-generated avatars and photo avatars that can deliver the narration in a talking-head format. A voice-cloning feature lets subscribers upload voice samples to create a custom synthetic voice for consistent, on-brand narration across a channel's content, and a translation and dubbing feature can localize an existing video into any of the 80-plus supported languages, which is a significant differentiator for teams targeting international audiences. Finished videos can be exported directly or, on paid plans, published straight to YouTube from within the app, and 'brand kits' let teams save fonts, colors, logos and templates so every generated video stays visually consistent.

Fliki's pricing follows a freemium subscription model with four public tiers. The Free plan requires no credit card and grants 36 credits per month, roughly equivalent to five minutes of finished content, capped at one-minute videos in 720p resolution, drawing from a smaller library of 300 standard voices, and exports carry a Fliki watermark, making it useful mainly for evaluation rather than production use. The Standard plan costs 14 dollars per month when billed annually, or 28 dollars per month when billed monthly, and includes 2,160 credits per year, videos up to 15 minutes long in full 1080p HD, access to 1,000 AI voices including 500 ultra-realistic voices, one custom voice clone, commercial usage rights, three brand kits, and the ability to publish up to 50 videos per month directly to YouTube, along with email and live-chat support. The Premium plan costs 44 dollars per month billed annually, or 88 dollars per month billed monthly, and raises the ceiling to 7,200 credits per year, videos up to 40 minutes long, access to more than 2,000 AI voices including over 1,000 ultra-realistic options, up to three voice clones, photo avatars and the full library of AI avatars, faster priority rendering, and priority customer support. At the top, the Enterprise plan uses custom, quote-based pricing billed annually and adds a custom credit allocation, bulk-purchase discounts, invoiced billing, API access for programmatic video generation, personalized and professional-grade voice cloning, fully branded templates, team collaboration tooling and a dedicated account manager, aimed at larger organizations or agencies producing video at scale.

Fliki's core audience spans three overlapping groups. Individual content creators and social media managers use it to turn scripts or trending topics into short-form video for platforms like YouTube Shorts, Instagram Reels and TikTok without needing filming equipment. Marketing teams and agencies use the blog-to-video workflow to repurpose existing written content, such as blog posts or product pages, into video assets for paid and organic distribution, leaning on brand kits to keep output consistent with existing visual identity. Educators, course creators and corporate training teams use the PPT-to-video and script-to-video modes, along with multilingual dubbing, to convert lesson decks or documentation into narrated video lessons that can reach non-English-speaking learners without re-recording from scratch.

In the competitive landscape, Fliki sits alongside a cluster of AI video and voice tools that emerged over the same period, including Synthesia and HeyGen, which focus more heavily on realistic AI presenter avatars for corporate and training video; Pictory and InVideo, which lean toward blog- and article-to-video repurposing similar to Fliki's own workflow; Descript, which blends video and audio editing with AI voice cloning in a more editor-centric interface; and Murf AI and ElevenLabs, which specialize primarily in text-to-speech and voice cloning rather than full video assembly. Fliki differentiates itself by combining text-to-speech, stock-visual selection, avatar generation, and multilingual translation into a single, script-first workflow explicitly optimized for speed, positioning itself as a generalist creation tool that spans several of these narrower categories rather than excelling narrowly in only one.

From a product-evolution standpoint, Fliki has steadily layered in more advanced generative capabilities since its 2021 founding, moving from a straightforward text-to-speech and stock-footage assembly tool toward a broader AI content suite that now includes voice cloning, AI avatars, photo avatars, an 'AI playground' for experimentation, and API access for developers who want to build automated content pipelines on top of the platform. This trajectory mirrors the broader AI content-creation market's shift from single-purpose generation tools toward integrated platforms that cover script generation, voice, visuals and distribution in one product, and it reflects Fliki's strategy of using a generous but limited free tier to convert a large evaluation audience, reported at over one million users, into a smaller base of paying Standard, Premium and Enterprise subscribers who need higher video-length ceilings, commercial rights, voice cloning or API access for scaled production.

Support and onboarding also factor into how Fliki positions itself against rivals. The Free and Standard tiers rely on self-serve documentation and email or live-chat support, while Premium subscribers get priority support queues, and Enterprise customers are assigned a dedicated account manager, a tiered support structure common among AI SaaS products moving upmarket from individual creators toward agency and enterprise buyers. The platform's frequently-asked-questions resources emphasize that credits, rather than a flat per-video fee, are the core unit of consumption, with longer videos, higher resolutions and premium ultra-realistic voices consuming credits faster than shorter, standard-voice content; this credit-based model is consistent with how comparable AI video and voice platforms meter usage, since the underlying generative inference cost scales with video length and voice quality rather than with a simple per-export count.

paragraphs: Fliki is an AI-powered text-to-video and text-to-speech platform, founded in 2021 by Sabir Ahmed and Atul Yadav, that converts scripts, blog URLs, ideas or slide decks into narrated videos using AI voices, stock visuals and optional AI avatars.

The platform is aimed at content creators, marketers and educators who want to produce video content quickly without filming, editing skills or studio equipment, and it reports serving over one million content creators.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features

It provides over 1,000 AI voices across more than 80 languages, voice cloning for branded narration, AI and photo avatars, multilingual translation and dubbing, brand kits for visual consistency, and direct publishing to YouTube.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Fliki uses a freemium, credit-based subscription model with a Free plan, plus Standard, Premium and Enterprise paid tiers. The Free plan includes 36 credits per month with 720p, watermarked, one-minute videos.

The Standard plan starts at 14 dollars per month billed yearly (28 dollars month-to-month), and the Premium plan starts at 44 dollars per month billed yearly (88 dollars month-to-month), while Enterprise pricing is custom and quote-based.

enrichment.short_description: Flipt is a git-native, self-hosted feature flag and feature management platform that stores flags as code in your own Git repositories so every change is a reviewable, auditable commit.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source with paid Pro and Enterprise tiers
enrichment.seo_title: Flipt Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flipt review 2026: a git-native, self-hosted feature flag platform. See how it works, Open Source vs Pro vs Enterprise pricing, key features and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Flipt is a git-native feature flag and feature management platform built for engineering teams that want to control application behavior through flags without handing operational control to a third-party SaaS vendor. Rather than storing flag state in a proprietary hosted database that only the vendor can see, Flipt treats feature flags as code: flag definitions live as structured files inside a team's own Git repository, and every toggle, rollout percentage change, or targeting rule update becomes a normal, reviewable Git commit that can go through the same pull request and code review process as any other code change. This design choice is the core differentiator that shapes almost everything else about the product, from its deployment model to its pricing philosophy to the audience it attracts.

The project originated as an open-source effort started by Mark Phelps, a software engineer who previously worked as a staff engineer at GitHub, where he helped build and scale the GitHub Container Registry. The flipt-io/flipt repository on GitHub was created in November 2016, and the project has been developed in the open since. Over time it evolved from a self-hosted server that used its own local or cloud database for flag storage into what the team now calls Flipt v2, a fully git-native architecture where the Git repository itself is the source of truth. This re-architecture reflects a broader philosophy: infrastructure and application configuration, including feature flags, should be managed the same way as the rest of a modern software stack, through version control, branches, and GitOps workflows rather than through a separate web dashboard with its own hidden state.

Technically, Flipt is written primarily in Go and compiles down to a single, statically linked binary with no required external runtime dependencies for the open-source edition, which is part of why it is popular for self-hosting in constrained environments such as Kubernetes sidecars, air-gapped networks, or edge deployments. The web-based management UI is a TypeScript and JavaScript single-page application that talks to the Go backend over REST and gRPC APIs. Flags can be organized into multiple environments using Git branches, separate directories within a repository, or entirely separate repositories, which lets teams mirror however they already structure environments such as staging and production. Flag evaluation results are pushed to connected client applications in near real time using Server-Sent Events streaming, so changes propagate in milliseconds without requiring clients to poll. Flipt ships official SDKs for a wide range of languages and runtimes, including Go, Python, JavaScript and browser JavaScript, Java, C#, Swift, Kotlin, and Dart, and it supports the vendor-neutral OpenFeature specification, having been an early adopter of the OpenFeature Remote Evaluation Protocol (OFREP). For authentication, Flipt supports OIDC, JWT, OAuth, Kubernetes service account tokens, and static API tokens, and its secrets management can be backed by a local file store in the open-source edition or by HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, or Azure Key Vault in paid tiers. On the source-control side, Flipt integrates with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps, and Gitea, allowing flag changes proposed through the UI to open real pull requests in a team's existing SCM rather than living in a silo.

Flipt's commercial model is unusually transparent for a developer infrastructure product. The open-source edition, licensed under the Fair Core License with an MIT Future License for the server (meaning the source converts to a permissive MIT license after a set time period) and a straightforward MIT license for client SDKs and libraries, is completely free and includes unlimited flags, full git-native workflows, real-time streaming updates, and both REST and gRPC APIs, with no artificial caps designed to force an upgrade. On top of that free foundation sits Flipt Pro, priced at 200 dollars per month with a 14-day free trial, which adds source-control-management integrations that let non-technical stakeholders propose flag changes from the UI that automatically become pull requests, GPG commit signing for cryptographic verification of who changed what, integrated secrets management against external vaults, and dedicated Slack-based support. Above that sits an Enterprise tier with custom, quote-based pricing that layers in advanced role-based access control, comprehensive audit logging, dedicated support relationships, and custom integrations for larger organizations with compliance requirements. Notably, as of August 2025 Flipt discontinued a previously offered hosted Flipt Cloud SaaS option, sharpening its focus back to the self-hosted open-source-plus-Pro-plus-Enterprise model rather than competing as a managed cloud service; teams that specifically want a zero-ops hosted feature flag SaaS are pointed elsewhere.

The target audience for Flipt is engineering teams, from individual developers and startups up through larger organizations, that are already comfortable operating self-hosted infrastructure and want feature flag management that fits naturally into an existing GitOps or infrastructure-as-code workflow. Because there is no proprietary hosted flag database to trust, and because the entire open-source core has no paid gate, Flipt is frequently chosen by teams for whom self-hosting, auditability, and avoiding vendor lock-in on business-critical toggle infrastructure are priorities, such as regulated industries, security-conscious engineering organizations, and platform teams building internal developer tooling. Companies referenced in Flipt's own marketing and case-study material as users of the platform include Rokt, which has publicly described using Flipt to dynamically enable features, implement bulkhead patterns for failure isolation, run production experiments, and maintain low latency and high availability, alongside references to larger technology company logos such as Nvidia and Cisco appearing in Flipt's promotional materials.

Within the broader feature-flag and feature-management category, Flipt competes most directly with other open-source or self-hostable options such as Unleash, GrowthBook, and Flagsmith, as well as with fully managed SaaS incumbents like LaunchDarkly, Split (now part of Harness), Statsig, and ConfigCat, and with feature-flag capabilities bundled into broader product analytics platforms such as PostHog. Flipt differentiates itself from the managed SaaS incumbents primarily through its self-hosted-first, git-native storage model, its single-binary zero-dependency deployment footprint, and a pricing structure where the free tier is functionally complete rather than a stripped-down trial. Relative to other open-source feature flag tools, Flipt's distinguishing bet is that Git, not a proprietary database, should be the system of record for flags, which appeals particularly to teams that already lean heavily on GitOps for infrastructure and want feature flags to inherit the same review, rollback, and audit trail guarantees as any other code change. As of mid-2026 the project continues to see active development, with the flipt-io/flipt GitHub repository having accumulated roughly 4,900 stars and regular releases, the latest of which in the v2.x line reflects continued investment in the git-native architecture, expanded SCM integrations, and enterprise-readiness features like RBAC and audit logging that make it viable for larger, compliance-conscious engineering organizations rather than only hobbyist or small-team use.

Flipt's licensing choice is itself part of its market positioning. The Fair Core License paired with an MIT Future License is a source-available model rather than a strictly OSI-approved open-source license, meaning the server code is publicly viewable and free to self-host and modify under defined terms, and automatically converts to the fully permissive MIT license after a set delay period, while client SDKs and libraries are MIT-licensed from day one. This approach is a deliberate middle path used by a number of modern developer-tooling companies to keep the day-to-day product experience fully open and self-hostable while still protecting the ability to build a sustainable Pro and Enterprise business around it, and it is a detail that technically-minded evaluators researching Flipt for procurement or compliance purposes typically want spelled out explicitly rather than assumed. In terms of product history, the shift from Flipt's earlier database-backed architecture to the git-native Flipt v2 model represented the single largest technical pivot in the project's lifetime, and it followed the broader industry trend of GitOps tooling, where Kubernetes manifests, infrastructure-as-code definitions, and now feature flags are all converging on Git as the common substrate for change management, review, and rollback. That pivot also coincided with Flipt narrowing its commercial focus after retiring its previously available hosted Flipt Cloud SaaS product, a sign that the team concluded a managed hosting business was a distraction from its core differentiation as the git-native alternative to database-backed feature flag platforms, rather than an attempt to compete head-on with fully managed incumbents like LaunchDarkly on hosting convenience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Flipt is a git-native feature flag management platform designed for engineering teams that want to control feature rollouts, experiments, and kill switches without giving up ownership of the underlying data. Instead of storing flag state in a vendor-controlled hosted database, Flipt stores flags as structured files inside a team's own Git repository, so every change to a flag becomes a reviewable, auditable Git commit that can flow through the same pull request process used for application code.

The project was started by Mark Phelps, a former GitHub staff engineer who helped build the GitHub Container Registry, and its GitHub repository has been developed in the open since 2016. Flipt compiles to a single Go binary with no required external dependencies for the open-source edition, making it well suited to self-hosted, air-gapped, or Kubernetes-based deployments where operators want full control over their feature flag infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Flipt's git-native architecture lets teams organize flags across environments using Git branches, directories, or separate repositories, and pushes evaluated flag changes to connected applications in near real time using Server-Sent Events streaming rather than requiring polling.

The platform ships official SDKs for Go, Python, JavaScript, Java, C#, Swift, Kotlin, and Dart, exposes both REST and gRPC APIs, and supports the vendor-neutral OpenFeature specification including the OpenFeature Remote Evaluation Protocol, so teams are not locked into Flipt-specific client libraries. Authentication options include OIDC, JWT, OAuth, and Kubernetes service tokens, and secrets can be stored locally or backed by HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, or Azure Key Vault on paid tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Flipt's open-source edition is entirely free, with no artificial limits on the number of flags, and includes full git-native workflows, real-time streaming updates, and both REST and gRPC APIs. Flipt Pro is priced at 200 dollars per month, includes a 14-day free trial, and adds source-control-management integrations, pull-request creation directly from the UI, GPG commit signing, external secrets management, and dedicated Slack support.

An Enterprise tier is available at custom, quote-based pricing for organizations that need advanced role-based access control, comprehensive audit logging, dedicated support, and custom integrations. As of August 2025, Flipt discontinued its previously available hosted Flipt Cloud offering, so the product is positioned squarely as a self-hosted platform with paid support and enterprise add-ons rather than a managed SaaS.

enrichment.short_description: Float is a resource management and team scheduling platform that helps agencies, studios, and professional services teams plan capacity, allocate work, and track project delivery in real time.
enrichment.seo_title: Float Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Float review 2026: resource management and team scheduling software for agencies. Compare Starter, Pro, and Enterprise pricing, key features, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Float is a resource management and team scheduling platform built to help agencies, professional services firms, studios, and in-house delivery teams plan who is working on what, when, and at what cost across every active project. The product's central metaphor is a visual, drag-and-drop schedule grid that shows every team member across a timeline, letting managers allocate hours to tasks and projects, track capacity and availability, log time off, and see at a glance where a team is overbooked or underutilized. Rather than trying to be a full project management suite, Float focuses narrowly and deeply on the resourcing and capacity-planning problem, which is the layer that sits above day-to-day task tracking and answers the higher-level question of whether a team has the people and hours available to deliver its committed work profitably.

The company traces its origin to 2011, when co-founders Glenn Rogers and Lars Gelfan met and bonded over a shared frustration at an advertising agency in New York City, where resource planning was handled with spreadsheets and constant improvisation, and where profitability and successful delivery often came down to luck rather than visibility. They set out to build a purpose-made tool for the problem, initially incorporating the business in New York under the name Pixel Paddock, LLC, with the product officially launching to the public on February 28, 2012, under the Float name. In the years since, the company has grown into a fully distributed, remote-first organization with team members spread across roughly 20 countries; co-founder Glenn Rogers himself relocated to his hometown of Melbourne, Australia in 2016, even as the company's registered headquarters has remained in New York City. Notably, Float has grown without ever raising traditional venture capital funding, a bootstrapped growth story that the founders and press coverage have pointed to as core to the company's culture and long-term independence. Float became a Certified B Corporation in March 2024, reflecting a formalized commitment to social and environmental accountability standards, and the platform is also GDPR and SOC 2 compliant, credentials that matter to the agency and enterprise customers who trust it with client and financial project data.

Technically, Float is delivered as a cloud-based SaaS web application, accessible through a browser-based scheduling interface as well as native mobile apps for iOS and Android that let managers and team members view and adjust the schedule while away from a desk. The core scheduling engine represents people, projects, and tasks on a shared timeline, with color-coded bars representing allocated work, and it layers on capacity views, time-off tracking, and utilization reporting so that managers can see rates of billable versus non-billable time across a team or individual. Float integrates with common workplace tools including Slack, for activity feed notifications, Jira, which lets teams drag and drop Jira issues directly onto the schedule and syncs project and task data, Google Calendar and Outlook Calendar for two-way calendar sync, and a range of additional connectors reachable through Zapier and similar automation platforms, alongside a documented API for teams that want to build custom integrations or pull scheduling data into their own reporting systems. Authentication support includes Google Sign-in and, on higher-tier plans, single sign-on and domain restrictions for centralized identity management. As project work progresses, Float layers in project-level financial tracking, including project margin tracking, rate cards for billing calculations, and, on the Pro plan and above, comparisons of project estimates against actuals so that agency leadership can see in near real time whether a given engagement is tracking toward or away from profitability.

Float's pricing is structured as a simple three-tier subscription priced per scheduled person per month, with monthly or annual billing options and no permanent free plan, though a free trial is available for teams evaluating the product. The Starter plan, at 7 dollars per person per month, covers the core of the product: drag-and-drop scheduling, capacity and time-off management, unlimited project scoping, project margin tracking, rate cards, one provisional resource for provisional planning, and a 7-day activity feed. The Pro plan, at 12 dollars per person per month, includes everything in Starter and adds project estimates, actuals tracking, estimates-versus-planned-versus-actuals reporting, delivery dashboards, client and currency rate cards, support for up to five concurrent multi-currency projects, single sign-on and domain restrictions, and five provisional resources. At the top, an Enterprise tier is available at custom, quote-based pricing for larger organizations, adding a dedicated Customer Success Manager, formal setup and training, priority support, service-level agreements and custom contract terms, manual payment options for organizations that cannot use self-serve credit card billing, unlimited multi-currency projects, and custom provisional allowances.

Float's target customers are principally agencies, in particular creative, marketing, and digital agencies, alongside architecture and engineering firms, consultancies, and other professional services businesses that sell people's time and need to keep utilization and delivery capacity under tight control, as well as in-house delivery and operations teams inside larger organizations that run project-based work. The company reports more than 4,500 customers globally and over 220,000 people actively scheduled through the platform, and it has been ranked the number-one resource management software on G2 for seven consecutive review seasons, carrying an aggregate rating around 4.3 out of 5 stars across more than 2,000 reviews at the time of writing. Within its category, Float is most often compared against Resource Guru, Runn, Mosaic, Productive.io, Harvest Forecast, and Kantata (formerly Mavenlink), as well as broader project management platforms such as monday.com and Asana that offer resourcing features as part of a larger suite. Float differentiates itself from those broader suites by staying deliberately focused on the scheduling and capacity layer rather than trying to also be a task tracker, invoicing tool, or full project management system, which reviewers consistently point to as both its biggest strength, in the form of a clean and fast interface purpose-built for the resourcing use case, and its main limitation, since teams that also want task-level project management typically need to pair Float with a separate tool such as Asana, Trello, or Jira.

Over its more than a decade of operation, Float's product evolution has tracked the growth of its core customer base from small creative agencies to larger, more complex, multi-currency and multi-office professional services organizations, which is reflected in the progressively more sophisticated financial and reporting features gated behind the Pro and Enterprise tiers, including estimates-versus-actuals reporting, delivery dashboards, and multi-currency project support. The company continues to describe its mission as modernizing resource management and supporting a sustainable future of remote and distributed work, a framing that lines up with its own fully remote operating structure, and it maintains active public-facing content, including a help center, an integrations marketplace, and a changelog documenting new features, all of which point to a maturing, independently run SaaS business rather than a product in an early or unstable stage of its life cycle.

Float's competitive position benefits from being a category specialist in a market where many buyers first evaluate broader project and work management suites such as monday.com, Asana, or Wrike before discovering that those tools' resourcing modules are typically thinner than a dedicated scheduling product. Reviewers and analysts covering the resource management space frequently note that Float's strength is speed of data entry and clarity of the visual schedule, since agency operations and resource managers often need to make dozens of small allocation adjustments per day and a slow or cluttered interface directly costs them time. The company's integrations strategy reinforces this specialist positioning: rather than trying to replace tools like Jira, Asana, or Trello for task-level work, Float is built to sit alongside them, pulling in tasks or issues from those systems and layering a capacity and scheduling view on top, with additional connectivity available through Zapier-style automation platforms and its own public API for teams that want custom reporting or two-way syncs with internal systems such as finance or HR tools. This complementary rather than competitive stance toward adjacent categories of software is a recurring theme in how Float is marketed and reviewed, and it helps explain why the company has been able to sustain a long run atop G2's resource management category rankings even as broader all-in-one work management platforms have added their own scheduling features over the same period.

Float was founded in 2011 by Glenn Rogers and Lars Gelfan in New York City, after the pair experienced firsthand how much resource planning at an advertising agency relied on spreadsheets and guesswork.
