enrichment.short_description: Float Financial is a Toronto-based fintech platform offering high-limit corporate credit cards, AI-assisted expense management, bill pay and high-yield business accounts built specifically for Canadian companies.
enrichment.seo_title: Float Financial Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Float Financial gives Canadian businesses corporate cards, expense management and high-yield accounts with no personal guarantee. See 2026 pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Float Financial, operating as Float Financial Solutions Inc. and commonly known simply as Float, is a Canadian fintech company that provides an integrated corporate card and business finance platform for companies incorporated in Canada. The product combines physical and virtual corporate credit cards, a high-yield CDIC-insured business account, automated expense management, bill payment and accounts payable tooling, employee reimbursements, and AI-assisted transaction coding into a single dashboard, positioning itself as a modern alternative to traditional Canadian bank business banking and corporate card products. Float is built to give finance teams real-time visibility into company spend rather than the delayed, statement-based reporting typical of legacy bank-issued corporate cards.

Float was founded in 2019 in Toronto by Rob Khazzam, who serves as Co-Founder and CEO and previously spent five years at Uber, alongside Co-Founder and Head of Product Ruslan Nikolaev, formerly of Uber ATG and Shopify, and Co-Founder Griffin Keglevich, who previously worked at KPMG and Shopify. The founding thesis was that Canadian financial institutions were not designed for the needs of small and medium-sized businesses, leaving companies with slow access to funds, limited visibility into spend, and outdated tools. The leadership team has since grown to include Tramale Turner as Chief Technology Officer, who previously worked at Stripe, Nintendo and Volkswagen, and Nina Bilimoria Angelo as SVP of Go-to-Market, bringing prior experience from HP, Pearson and Top Hat.

Technically, Float issues unlimited physical and virtual CAD and USD corporate cards to employees, each governed by custom, admin-set spending controls such as per-card limits, merchant category restrictions and expiration rules. Credit limits can scale up to 3,000,000 dollars CAD provided the business holds sufficient cash in its connected Float account to fund spending, and the company does not require personal guarantees or personal credit checks from founders or executives to open an account, a meaningful difference from most traditional Canadian bank corporate card programs. The Float mobile app allows employees to automatically capture and match receipts to transactions, while Float Intelligence, the platform's AI transaction coding agent, automatically assigns general ledger and tax codes to expenses, a feature Float states achieves over 95 percent coding precision and outperforms general-purpose large language models by roughly 30 percent on the same task. On the banking side, Float offers a CDIC-insured business account that can earn up to 3.5 percent interest with no monthly account fees, fee-free inbound payments via wire, EFT and ACH, and foreign exchange conversion between CAD and USD at rates the company advertises as substantially lower than traditional bank markups. The platform connects directly to QuickBooks, Xero and NetSuite for accounting synchronization, and also supports custom exports and API access for finance teams with bespoke workflows.

Float's pricing is structured across three tiers. The Essentials plan is permanently free and includes up to 20 physical cards plus unlimited virtual cards with custom limits and controls, the CDIC-insured interest-bearing account, fee-free EFT, ACH and wire payments, 0.25 percent FX rates, mobile receipt capture, employee reimbursements with direct CAD payouts, and accounting sync with QuickBooks, Xero or custom exports. The Professional plan costs 10 dollars CAD per user per month, with active-user pricing starting at 100 dollars per month covering the first 10 users, and adds unlimited physical and virtual cards with advanced controls, multi-level request and approval workflows, advanced NetSuite integration, the Float Intelligence coding agent, international expense reporting, and USD direct payouts. The Enterprise plan uses custom, contact-sales pricing and layers on custom implementation and data migration support, dedicated onboarding and training workshops, a dedicated Customer Success Manager, and custom credit terms with dedicated underwriting for larger credit needs.

Float's target customers span solopreneurs and freelancers through startups, small and mid-sized businesses, and mid-market companies, with particular traction among non-profits, agencies, e-commerce businesses and hospitality operators. The company states it serves more than 7,000 companies, including named customers such as Impact Kitchen, Ocean Wise, Cohere and Klue. Because Float is a Canadian-only product tied to CDIC insurance and FINTRAC registration as a money services business, it is not usable by companies outside Canada, which sets it apart from cross-border competitors.

In the competitive landscape, Float competes most directly with other corporate card and spend management platforms operating in or expanding into Canada, including Jeeves, Loop, Brim Financial and Plooto, as well as U.S.-founded players such as Ramp and Brex that have limited or no native Canadian presence, and traditional bank-issued corporate card programs from institutions like RBC, TD, BMO and American Express Business. Float differentiates itself through its Canadian-first regulatory posture, including CDIC insurance and bilingual English and French support, the absence of personal guarantees or credit checks to open an account, materially higher interest yield on idle cash than most competitors, and its AI-driven transaction coding feature aimed at reducing manual bookkeeping work for finance teams.

Float has raised more than 300 million dollars CAD in total funding, including an 85 million dollar Series C round, from investors including Inovia Capital, Goldman Sachs Alternatives, OMERS Ventures, Silicon Valley Bank and BDC Capital. Coverage from The Globe and Mail reported the company reaching a valuation of approximately 548 million dollars following its most recent funding round, and Float was recognized as one of the fastest-growing fintech companies on The Globe and Mail's Top Growing Companies list for 2025, later cited among Canada's top growing startups heading into 2026.

Architecturally, Float operates as a cloud-based platform accessible through a web dashboard and native iOS and Android mobile apps, with card issuing and payment rails built on top of regulated banking and card network infrastructure appropriate for a FINTRAC-registered money services business. The platform's API allows finance and engineering teams to pull transaction and account data into internal systems or custom reporting tools beyond the native QuickBooks, Xero and NetSuite integrations.

Product-wise, Float has evolved from an initial focus on corporate cards into a broader business finance suite, adding the high-yield business account, bill pay and accounts payable automation, employee reimbursement workflows, and most recently AI-powered transaction coding through Float Intelligence. The company's 2026 launch of a dedicated USD corporate card, marketed as Canada's smartest corporate card for USD spend, reflects continued expansion into cross-border spend management for Canadian businesses that transact internationally, reinforcing Float's strategy of building a full-stack alternative to piecing together a bank account, a separate card provider, and third-party expense software.

Onboarding for a new Float customer typically begins with incorporating documentation review consistent with FINTRAC anti-money-laundering requirements, since Float operates as a registered money services business rather than a chartered bank, with underlying deposit-taking and card-issuing functions handled through regulated banking partners rather than by Float itself holding a banking license directly. This partner-bank model is common among modern fintech card issuers globally and allows Float to move faster on product development than a traditional chartered bank while still offering CDIC deposit insurance protection on funds held in Float accounts, a detail the company emphasizes prominently in its marketing given how important deposit safety is to finance leaders evaluating a newer, non-bank provider. Once approved, a company can typically issue its first virtual card within minutes rather than the days or weeks often associated with traditional bank corporate card applications, a speed advantage Float attributes directly to its cash-secured underwriting model that avoids traditional credit bureau checks and manual underwriting review for most applicants.

Float's customer support model scales with plan tier, with Essentials customers relying on self-serve help documentation and standard email support, Professional customers gaining access to more responsive support channels, and Enterprise customers receiving a named Customer Success Manager alongside implementation assistance for migrating historical expense and accounting data from a prior card provider or bank. The company also publishes its own in-house content and calculators, such as tools comparing the cost of Float's corporate cards against traditional bank fees, as part of a broader content marketing strategy aimed at Canadian finance leaders who are actively comparing spend management options, a strategy that mirrors how larger international competitors such as Ramp and Brex have built demand-generation engines around finance-team education content in their respective markets.

faqs.question: What is Float Financial?
faqs.answer: Float Financial is a Canadian fintech platform that provides corporate credit cards, a high-yield business account, expense management, bill pay and AI-assisted transaction coding for businesses incorporated in Canada.

faqs.question: Is Float Financial free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Float's Essentials plan is permanently free and includes up to 20 physical cards, unlimited virtual cards, the interest-bearing account and core expense features. Paid Professional and Enterprise tiers add advanced controls and integrations.

faqs.question: Does Float require a personal guarantee or credit check?
faqs.answer: No, Float does not require a personal guarantee or a personal credit check to open an account. Credit limits are instead tied to the cash balance held in the connected Float account.

faqs.question: What accounting software does Float integrate with?
faqs.answer: Float integrates directly with QuickBooks, Xero and NetSuite, and also supports custom exports and API access for businesses with bespoke accounting workflows.

faqs.question: Is Float available outside Canada?
faqs.answer: No, Float is built specifically for businesses incorporated in Canada and is registered as a money services business with FINTRAC; it is not available to companies outside Canada.

faqs.question: How does Float's AI transaction coding work?
faqs.answer: Float Intelligence, Float's AI transaction coding agent, automatically assigns general ledger and tax codes to card transactions, which the company states achieves over 95% precision and reduces manual bookkeeping effort.

enrichment.short_description: Flock is a team messaging and collaboration app offering channel-based chat, video calls, shared to-dos and third-party app integrations, marketed as an affordable Slack alternative for businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: Flock Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flock is a team messaging app with chat, video calls and shared to-dos, positioned as an affordable Slack alternative. See 2026 pricing and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Flock is a proprietary team messaging and collaboration platform that combines channel-based chat, direct messaging, video conferencing, screen and file sharing, and a suite of built-in productivity tools such as shared to-dos, polls and reminders into a single workspace. Marketed heavily as the number one Slack alternative, Flock is aimed at businesses that want enterprise-style team communication without the higher per-user cost typically associated with Slack and similar platforms.

Flock was founded in 2014 by tech entrepreneur Bhavin Turakhia, who had previously co-founded the Directi group of internet businesses along with his brother Divyank Turakhia. Flock launched as part of that broader Directi portfolio, which over the years has included companies such as Radix, Zeta, CodeChef and, later, the email service Titan. Directi's business units are described as being run independently by their own management teams while sharing a common culture and founder lineage, and Flock followed that model as a standalone messaging product rather than an internal Directi tool. Coverage at launch described Flock's ambition to be a WhatsApp-style messaging experience purpose-built for workplace use rather than personal chat.

Technically, Flock organizes communication around teams, which can represent an entire company or a subset of it, and within each team users create public channels that are open for discovery and private channels that require an invitation. Direct messages and group messages support unlimited 1-1 and group conversations. Flock's video conferencing supports both one-on-one and group calls with screen sharing, and the platform includes a set of native productivity apps out of the box, including polls, shared to-do lists, a Mailcast feature for broadcast-style updates, code snippet sharing aimed at engineering teams, reminders, and note sharing. FlockOS is the platform's extensibility layer, giving developers a framework to build custom apps and integrations on top of Flock, and this powers the Flock App Store, which offers pre-built integrations with tools such as Google Drive, Google Analytics, Trello, GitHub, Twitter, Mailchimp, Zapier, Asana, Zoom and Jira. Administrators get controls over authentication, data retention and access, and user permissions, and larger customers can layer on Active Directory sync and single sign-on.

Flock's pricing follows a three-tier freemium structure. The Starter plan is free for teams of 1 to 20 members and includes unlimited 1-1 and group messaging, up to 10,000 searchable messages, 10 public channels, 5 GB of total team storage, a single team admin role, and 1-1 video calls. The Pro plan is designed for teams of 20 to 100 members and costs 6 dollars per user per month billed monthly, dropping to 4.50 dollars per user per month when billed annually; it comes with a 30-day free trial that does not require a credit card and adds unlimited searchable messages, unlimited public and private channels, 10 GB of storage per team member, multiple team admins, group video calls with screen sharing, and 24/7 priority support. The Enterprise plan targets organizations with more than 100 members and uses custom, contact-sales pricing, adding organizational mapping and custom onboarding, 20 GB of storage per team member, Active Directory sync, single sign-on, and 24/7 dedicated support. Flock also offers a guest policy in which multi-channel guests can participate for free for 90 days from the date of invitation, after which standard member rates apply.

Flock's target customers span cross-functional teams inside small and mid-sized businesses through larger enterprises that need admin controls, compliance features and dedicated support. The company lists customers including Britannia, Unacademy, Namecheap and Sodexo, and positions the product for use across engineering, sales, marketing, product and HR and customer support teams within an organization.

In the team messaging market, Flock competes most directly with Slack and Microsoft Teams, as well as smaller Slack-alternative players such as Chanty, Troop Messenger, Rocket.Chat and Google Chat. Flock differentiates primarily on price, positioning its Pro plan at roughly half the per-user cost of Slack's comparable paid tier, and on its bundle of native productivity apps such as to-dos, polls and reminders, which reduces the need to stitch together separate task-management tools alongside the chat product. Its generous free tier, supporting up to 20 team members with unlimited 1-1 and group messaging, is also positioned as more usable out of the box for small teams than some competitors' free plans.

Flock has received industry recognition since its early years, including the Best Business Communication App award at the Global Mobile App Summit and Awards in July 2016 and the Best Mobile Enterprise Product or Service award at the India Digital Awards in 2017. The company's legal entity, Flock FZ-LLC, is registered in a Dubai free zone, while the product's primary operations and engineering base have most commonly been associated with Mumbai, India, reflecting Flock's roots in the broader Directi and Titan family of companies founded by Bhavin Turakhia.

Architecturally, Flock is available as native desktop applications for Windows and macOS, mobile apps for Android and iOS, a browser-based web client, and a Chrome extension, giving teams flexible access across devices. The FlockOS platform and App Store function as the extensibility backbone that lets Flock connect into a company's existing toolchain rather than requiring teams to abandon tools they already use for project management, file storage or marketing analytics.

Since its 2014 launch, Flock has evolved from a straightforward team chat app into a broader collaboration suite, adding video conferencing, an app marketplace through FlockOS, and enterprise-grade administration and security features such as SSO and Active Directory sync to compete for larger accounts. Its continued marketing emphasis as the number one Slack alternative reflects a strategy of competing on affordability and an integrated feature set rather than trying to out-scale Slack's ecosystem of third-party integrations directly, making Flock most attractive to price-sensitive small and mid-sized teams as well as organizations that want core productivity apps bundled directly into their messaging tool.

Flock's engineering and design approach has historically emphasized speed and a low-friction onboarding experience, letting a new team sign up, invite members, and start messaging within minutes without a lengthy configuration process, a deliberate contrast to some enterprise collaboration suites that require significant IT involvement before rollout. The mobile apps for Android and iOS mirror the desktop feature set closely, including channel messaging, video calling, and access to the shared to-do lists and reminders, so that distributed and field-based teams are not limited to a stripped-down mobile experience. Flock also supports message search, file sharing with previews for common document types, and @mentions and threaded replies within channels, features that align it functionally with the baseline expectations users bring from competing chat products, while its bundled productivity apps are positioned as the differentiator that reduces the total number of separate tools a small team needs to subscribe to.

From a business-model standpoint, Flock's continued operation as a proprietary, privately held product under the Directi-linked corporate structure, rather than as a venture-funded standalone startup chasing rapid user growth at a loss, has allowed it to maintain a stable, moderately priced offering over more than a decade without the aggressive pricing swings or feature deprecations sometimes seen in venture-backed SaaS products under pressure to reach profitability. This stability, combined with steady incremental feature additions like the FlockOS app marketplace and expanded enterprise administration tooling, has let Flock sustain a loyal base of small and mid-sized business customers even as much larger, better-funded competitors like Slack and Microsoft Teams have dominated enterprise-level market share and analyst attention in the broader team collaboration category.

Founded in 2014 by Bhavin Turakhia as part of the broader Directi group of companies, Flock is positioned as the number one Slack alternative, emphasizing lower per-user pricing and a bundled feature set.

Teams on Flock are organized into public channels, which are open for discovery, and private channels that require an invitation, giving organizations flexibility over how information flows across departments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Flock combines unlimited 1-1 and group messaging with 1-1 and group video calls with screen sharing, plus native productivity apps including shared to-dos, polls, reminders, note sharing and code snippet sharing built specifically for technical teams.

FlockOS is Flock's extensibility platform, powering the Flock App Store, which integrates with tools such as Google Drive, Trello, GitHub, Zapier, Asana, Zoom and Jira. Enterprise customers also get admin controls covering authentication, data retention and access, user permissions, Active Directory sync and single sign-on.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Flock's Starter plan is free for teams of up to 20 members, with unlimited 1-1 and group messaging, 10,000 searchable messages, 10 public channels and 5 GB of team storage. The Pro plan, for teams of 20 to 100 members, costs 6 dollars per user per month billed monthly or 4.50 dollars per user per month billed annually, and includes a 30-day free trial with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What is Flock used for?
faqs.answer: Flock is used as a team messaging and collaboration app for internal company communication, combining channel-based chat, direct messaging, video calls and built-in productivity tools like to-dos and polls.

faqs.question: Is Flock free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Flock offers a free Starter plan for teams of up to 20 members with unlimited 1-1 and group messaging, 10,000 searchable messages and 10 public channels. Paid Pro and Enterprise plans unlock unlimited history, more channels and admin controls.

faqs.question: Who founded Flock?
faqs.answer: Flock was founded in 2014 by tech entrepreneur Bhavin Turakhia, who also co-founded the Directi group of internet businesses and later founded Titan.

faqs.question: How does Flock compare to Slack?
faqs.answer: Flock is marketed as an affordable Slack alternative, with its Pro plan priced at roughly half of Slack's comparable per-user cost and a bundle of native productivity apps like to-dos, polls and reminders included by default.

faqs.question: Does Flock offer video calling?
faqs.answer: Yes, Flock supports 1-1 video calls on its free Starter plan and group video calls with screen sharing on its Pro and Enterprise plans.

faqs.question: Can Flock integrate with other business tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, through FlockOS and the Flock App Store, Flock integrates with tools such as Google Drive, Google Analytics, Trello, GitHub, Zapier, Asana, Zoom and Jira.
